summary: I was born at St. Anthony’s in Oklahoma City, and my family has deep roots there. My mother, Katie, grew up in Oklahoma City and attended Capitol Hill High School. My grandfather and grandmother lived in Oklahoma City until they passed away, so the city has always been a significant part of my family history.

My grandfather was named Lon Chaney, but not the famous actor Lon Chaney. He was part of my family lineage, and I always felt a connection to him through my mother’s side. Though he wasn’t the Hollywood star, he was an important figure in my family’s story.

My mother went to Capitol Hill High School in Oklahoma City, where she grew up before meeting my father. It was part of her Oklahoma City upbringing, and that’s where their paths crossed before they married. Capitol Hill High School is a key part of my mother’s early life.

My father was from Blanchard, Oklahoma, and he served two hitches in the war before meeting my mother. After they married and I was born, we moved around a bit, but Blanchard was where my dad originally came from. It’s a small town that shaped his early life before the war.

My dad started a propane business out in Coweta County after working with his uncle. While we lived in Binger, Oklahoma, he was busy running that business and delivering propane to irrigation wells and homes. Coweta County was important to our family’s livelihood and my dad’s work ethic.

I am part Choctaw, about one-sixteenth, though we never got officially on the rolls. My grandfather Clay Bench’s mother was a full-blood Choctaw, but the family was fostered out and didn’t register. Still, I’ve always been proud of my Choctaw heritage and it’s a meaningful part of who I am.

When I was three years old, we moved to Binger, Oklahoma, which is about fifty-five to sixty miles southwest of Oklahoma City and west of Norman. It was in Binger that my dad worked in the propane business and started our family’s journey in baseball. Binger was the backdrop for my early childhood and sports beginnings.
cues: location relative to Norman and Oklahoma City

summary: Norman, Oklahoma, is near where I grew up; Binger is west of Norman. It was part of the region where I played baseball and developed my skills. Though I didn’t live in Norman, it was close enough to be part of the local baseball scene and community.

I started playing American Legion Baseball at age fourteen, competing with seventeen- and eighteen-year-olds. I played for a team in Anadarko, Oklahoma, and even hit a home run to beat Bobby Murcer’s American Legion team. It was a crucial stage in my baseball development, where I pitched, caught, and played multiple positions.
cues: home run against Bobby Murcer’s team

summary: I played American Legion Baseball in Anadarko, Oklahoma, which is the county seat of Coweta County. There, I had to compete for catching time because they had three catchers, but I eventually got my chance and made an impact. Anadarko was an important place for my early baseball career.

I remember hitting a home run against Bobby Murcer’s American Legion team when I was playing in Anadarko. That was a memorable moment because it helped us win the game, and Bobby Murcer was a notable opponent even then. It was one of those early highlights that showed me I could compete with top players.

The Chicago Cubs were very interested in me when I was coming up as a player. Billy Caps, their scout, loved my skills and wanted to draft me in the first round of the 1965 draft, which was the first ever draft. However, the Cubs decided not to use a first-round pick on me because they thought I wasn’t well known enough at the time.

Billy Caps was the Chicago Cubs scout who really liked me and wanted to draft me in the first round back in 1965. He even brought the head of scouting down to watch me play, and after a strong game, he was convinced I was a great prospect. Unfortunately, the Cubs decided not to use a first-round pick on me despite his support.

Jim McLaughlin was the Minor League director for the Cubs who was part of the discussions about me. He was involved in scouting and player decisions during that time. His opinion was part of the evaluation process as the Cubs considered drafting me.

Tony Robello was mentioned in the context of baseball scouting and development, though I didn’t have a direct personal story about him in this part of my history. He was part of the baseball world during my early career and scouting days.

Bob Thurman was another baseball figure mentioned during discussions about scouting and player development. Like Tony Robello, he was part of the baseball environment I was involved in as I progressed toward the professional level.

The Cincinnati Reds were the team I eventually came up to in the majors, and they called me 'The Little General' because they wanted a leader. My time with the Reds was significant as it marked the realization of my dream to play major league baseball. The Reds organization recognized my leadership and skills, which was a proud moment for me.
